metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, and paper type match. Scope is correct: municipal support/opposition to Swiss taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy, including one with revenue recycling.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper. Metadata are correct and the study is clearly tax-specific. The main issue is completeness: three sectoral exposure determinants are missing, and several extracted covariates are better labeled as controls.

metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, paper type, and tax scope all match; the paper squarely studies carbon-tax and fossil-fuel-tax support.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict tax corpus. Most extracted determinants are supported and tax-specific. Remove the climate-impacts treatment row as too broad for a tax-determinant table, and consider adding direct fuel-exposure correlates such as car dependence and high gas expenses.

metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, and review type match. The paper is broad climate-policy review, but it includes multiple direct carbon/fuel-tax acceptability studies, so tax scope is correct.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict tax corpus. Most extracted determinants are supported, but three entries are not tax-specific enough, and the extraction misses the review's explicit higher-costs-negative-support determinant.

metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, paper type, and Sweden carbon-tax/fuel-tax scope all match the paper.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper. It is a direct carbon-tax acceptability study, and the extraction is tax-specific; the only non-core items are standard demographic/political controls.

metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, and paper type match; the paper contains an explicit global GHG/carbon tax support analysis, so tax scope is correct.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep in corpus. The extraction contains only tax-specific determinants directly tied to GCS support, and all listed determinants are supported by the paper.

The paper is correctly in the direct CO2-tax acceptability corpus. Main tax-specific fairness and car-use determinants are captured; demographic/political variables should be treated as controls, and one tax-related treatment variable from Table 3 is missing.

metadata_comments: Title/DOI/year/paper type are correct. Tax scope is mixed: the relevant outcome is support for carbon tax/cap-and-trade, not a tax-only estimate.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep. The extraction contains one directly relevant determinant and no extraneous tax-irrelevant predictors; note that the policy outcome is pooled carbon tax/cap-and-trade support, not a pure carbon-tax estimate.

metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, and empirical survey classification are correct. The paper also studies road pricing and parking fees, but it clearly contains direct fuel-tax support analysis.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict fuel-tax corpus. The three core fuel-tax determinants are correctly captured: perceived effectiveness, distributional concerns, and personal-finance impacts. However, several control variables were listed as determinants and should be separated into controls rather than main results.

metadata_comments: Tax scope is acceptable: the paper directly measures support for a 60-cent gasoline tax and a tax-policy index that includes gasoline and business energy taxes.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict tax corpus. The extraction is broadly accurate, but it should flag political/demographic variables as controls and add the two omitted tax-policy controls: Female and Member of environmental groups.
Keep the paper in the strict carbon-tax corpus. The CT-support determinants are correctly extracted, but age should be reclassified as a demographic control rather than a determinant.

metadata_comments: Metadata match the article. It is a systematic review centered on carbon taxes/CO2 taxes and related fuel-tax evidence, even though it also reviews cap-and-trade.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the corpus. Metadata are correct and the extracted items are tax-linked, but the extraction is incomplete: several direct carbon-tax acceptability determinants from Tables 2 and 4 are missing, especially comfort/wellbeing, voluntary-behaviour concerns, burden-sharing, rural households, future generations, and infrastructure spending.

Keep the paper. Main direct determinants are partisanship and distributional fairness framing. Add the personal-fairness framing if exhaustive coverage of directly tested carbon-tax determinants is desired.

metadata_comments: Title, DOI, year, and paper type match. Tax scope is appropriate for direct carbon/fuel-tax support, with subsidy reform used only as comparative context.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the corpus. The extraction is substantively on target, but several reported coefficients are left as n.a. despite clear positive/negative signs, and Europe and West Asia is missing.
The extraction is tax-specific and retains the carbon-tax determinants. No missing carbon-tax determinants were identified; demographic and regional variables are acceptable as controls/context.

reason_for_exclusion_if_any: The paper measures support for a gasoline-tax item only as part of a broader climate-policy support index; it does not estimate gasoline-tax-specific determinants.
metadata_comments: The gasoline tax appears only as one item in a composite policy battery. The reported regressions predict the overall index, not a gasoline-tax-specific outcome.
final_recommendation_summary: Exclude from the strict tax corpus: no determinant results are reported for a direct carbon-tax or fuel-tax outcome, only for a mixed climate-policy support index that includes gasoline tax.

metadata_comments: Title, paper type, and tax scope match the supplied record. No DOI or publication year are stated in the provided materials.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep. The extraction contains only direct carbon-tax correlates from Topic 11, and no additional supported carbon/fuel-tax determinants are evident.

metadata_comments: Title, year, and paper type are correct. DOI is absent as expected. Core scope is a Swiss referendum on fossil/non-renewable energy taxes, with subsidy proposals as secondary context.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict tax corpus. Metadata are correct. The extraction needs cleanup: remove or separate subsidy-proposal determinants (especially language and earmarked subsidies) and retain only the tax-relevant recycling item for the green tax reform. Household income should be added as a secondary direct determinant.

Keep the paper. Main carbon/fuel-tax determinants are well covered, but demographic and political correlates should be reclassified as controls, one personal-cost item is redundant, and coerciveness/push-pull framing should be added.

metadata_comments: Title matches the PDF. No DOI or publication year is visible in the provided preprint text. Paper type and direct carbon/fuel-tax scope are correct.
final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict tax corpus. The extraction is tax-specific and captures the carbon-tax support model, but standard demographic/political variables should be labeled as controls rather than substantive determinants.
